one_line_takeaway: A qualitative DFD/STRIDE threat model of an LLM-enabled edge-cloud robot shows that conventional-cyber, adversarial-ML, and prompt-injection threats converge at the same six architectural boundary crossings, and traces three published-attack-grounded chains (direct injection, visual injection, provider-mediated tool-call injection) that all terminate in unsafe physical actuation because no component independently validates LLM-generated plans before dispatch.
key_finding: The three threat categories (CCT, AdvT, ConT) do not partition cleanly by boundary crossing but instead converge at the same crossings -- e.g., crossing (v), the edge-robot wireless link, simultaneously hosts wireless MITM (CCT), adversarial telemetry perturbation (AdvT), and visual-prompt-injection content propagating in from crossing (vi) (ConT). Tracing the three attack chains surfaces three distinct architectural root causes: Chain 1 exposes the absence of a 'semantic firewall' -- no component between the User Interface (P1) and the Autonomous Platform (P6) independently validates plan safety, so the entire safety burden rests on the cloud LLM's own alignment; Chain 2 exposes cross-modal translation -- a visual perturbation that would be bounded to misclassification in a conventional perception pipeline is escalated into full plan-level compromise because the vision encoder's output is consumed as textual LLM context; Chain 3 exposes an unmediated boundary crossing -- the E5<->E4 provider-mediated tool-call exchange occurs entirely outside TB1's observation, so the edge system has no record that external content was consulted. A separate cross-cutting finding is that repudiation threats recur at every crossing due to a single root cause: no component (P1, P2, P3, P5, or P6) maintains cryptographically signed, integrity-protected logs of commands, plans, prompts, or sensor data, and neither E5 nor E3 provide any cryptographic provenance -- meaning the system cannot forensically attribute an unsafe physical action to a specific instruction, plan, sensor input, or network manipulation after an incident. The paper also notes a temporal-persistence concern: adversarial content written into State/Session Memory (D1) or Prompt and Policy Assets (D3) can convert a one-shot injection into a recurring compromise across future planning cycles.
core_contribution: The paper presents a systematic, DFD-based STRIDE-per-interaction threat model of an LLM-enabled autonomous robot deployed in an edge-cloud architecture. It builds a hierarchical Data Flow Diagram (Level 0 and Level 1) around two trust boundaries (TB1: edge server; TB2: autonomous platform) and six boundary-crossing interaction points, and elicits threats at each crossing using a three-category taxonomy — Conventional Cyber Threats (CCT, anchored in MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise), Adversarial Threats (AdvT, anchored in MITRE ATLAS), and Conversational Threats (ConT, anchored in the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications). The authors' own claimed contributions are: (1) a hierarchical system model of the edge-cloud architecture (five edge processes P1-P5, three data stores D1-D3, autonomous platform P6, five external entities E1-E5); (2) systematic threat elicitation across all six boundary-crossing interactions using the three-category taxonomy; and (3) tracing of three specific cross-boundary attack chains from external entry points through the perception-planning-actuation pipeline to unsafe physical actuation, each exposing a distinct architectural property (absence of independent semantic validation, cross-modal translation of visual input into LLM instructions, and unmediated boundary crossing via provider-side tool use).
threat_model: Adversary is characterized by six capability classes, explicitly scoped in the Methods section: (1) a network-adjacent adversary able to observe/inject/modify traffic on wireless or network links (enabling MITM, replay, injection); (2) a malicious or compromised external service returning crafted content via direct invocation or provider-mediated tool use, enabling indirect prompt injection/context poisoning; (3) a compromised perception source supplying manipulated visual input via external cameras or on-board sensors (e.g., adversarial patches, projected light); (4) a malicious or unwitting user submitting crafted natural-language instructions (direct prompt injection, jailbreaking, role-play framing); (5) a hardware-tampering adversary with sustained physical access who inserts an interposer between sensor modules and onboard compute (e.g., on the MIPI CSI bus, citing Liu et al.'s demonstrated attack); and (6) the cloud-hosted LLM service itself, treated as untrusted since its outputs may be unsafe, incorrect, or adversarially influenced. Explicit scoping assumptions exclude LLM training-data poisoning/model supply-chain attacks (third-party model, no control over weights/training data), multi-robot coordination, and adversaries with pre-existing privileged code execution, nation-state capabilities, or physical-destruction goals. Security objectives extend the classical CIA triad with a fourth objective, safety, on the grounds that compromise can propagate to physical-world consequences (unsafe movement, collisions, equipment damage).
limitations: The authors explicitly state several boundaries: (1) the analysis is purely architectural/qualitative -- no attacks were actually implemented or run, and no physical robotic platform was used for empirical validation (listed as a 'natural next step'); (2) scope excludes LLM training-data poisoning and model supply-chain attacks, multi-robot coordination, and adversaries with pre-existing privileged access; (3) the perception analysis focuses on the visual channel, explicitly leaving audio and other modalities for future extension of the same methodology; (4) the paper's own Discussion section notes that ATT&CK, ATLAS, and the OWASP LLM Top 10 each cover only one architectural layer and that no single taxonomy captures their intersection, calling a unified taxonomy for LLM-enabled cyber-physical systems an open problem; (5) the authors position the work explicitly as 'not a new threat-modeling methodology but a systematic application of DFD-based STRIDE' to a previously unanalyzed integrated setting -- i.e., they do not claim methodological novelty, only novelty of the analysis target and integration; (6) platform-specific severity shifts (e.g., aerial vs. ground platforms, co-located vs. edge-cloud LLM inference) are acknowledged as requiring re-evaluation by practitioners rather than being resolved in the paper.
